preamble: While the schwa, a mid-central vowel, is typically associated with vowel reduction in unstressed syllables, in some specific linguistic contexts, it can undergo a process of strengthening and diphthongization. This transformation involves the expansion of the reduced vowel into a more complex, often fronted and raised diphthong, such as /ie/. This shift represents a reversal of the more common reduction processes, indicating a re-emphasis or specific phonological conditioning that promotes the development of a fuller vowel sound from a previously minimized one.
phoneticEffects: Diphthongization, Vowel Strengthening, Fronting, Raising, Vowel Expansion

examples.note: Proto-Utupua-Vanikoro word-final *apë becomes n-epie in Vano.
Proto-Utupua-Vanikoro word-final *upë becomes upie in Vano.
Proto-Utupua-Vanikoro word-final *topë becomes lepie in Vano.
Proto-Utupua-Vanikoro word-final *ma[g]ulipë becomes milipie in Vano.

sources: Ross, M. (2009). An Oceanic Origin for Äiwoo, the Language of the Reef Islands? Oceanic Linguistics, 48(2), 437-465.
